response:
The `/decide` process is complete: framed the scope, ran an opposing-counsel advisor panel (process-economist, lean-process-engineer, session-operator), had the verifier reproduce every load-bearing textual claim, and recorded the decision as **ADR 0035**. No safety BLOCKER caveat was folded in, so red-team/tech-lead were not required. No code changed, so no version bump applies (meta/tooling exempt).

Here is the final decision record:

---

```
---
id: 0035
title: "ISSUE-41 tiered-agent experiment: run full pre-registered grid, no arm-level early-stop"
status: accepted
summary: "Reject piloting Opus-solo first and stopping early: the primary gate (delta+tokens vs Sonnet-solo) is undecidable from an Opus-solo-only pilot, and Haiku-solo/Opus-solo are load-bearing for two other pre-registered deliverables (the 4-arm frontier, the Opus-quality-at-sub-cost report). Run the full 4-arm x 8-task x 3-rep grid; the only pre-sanctioned cost control is the already-registered rep/task-level sequential escalation — no new arm-skipping mechanism."
---

# 0035 — ISSUE-41 execution design: full grid, no arm-level early-stop

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: opposing counsel — process-economist (pro pilot-first/cost) vs lean-process-engineer (pro full-grid/poka-yoke), plus session-operator (execution-realism, unassigned). Verifier reproduced all 5 load-bearing textual claims fresh.
- Context: ISSUE-41.md's pre-registration (Comment 1, lines 25-59) specifies a 4-arm x 8-task x 3-rep grid with a rep/task-level sequential-escalation clause (line 49). This session's execution-design call: pilot the most expensive arm (Opus-solo) first and stop early if the adopt/reject gate is already decidable, to cut spend before the full ~250-300-invocation run (Comment 3, line 71).

## Decision
Run the FULL pre-registered grid (4 arms x 8 tasks x 3 reps), no arm-level early-stop. The pre-registration's own sequential-escalation clause (line 49: add reps/tasks only where a cell's CI straddles the boundary) is the sole sanctioned cost control — it operates within a running arm, never by skipping or deferring a whole arm. The document's only pre-sanctioned deferral is Opus-MAIN (a Tiered variant, line 37/67), not a solo arm.

## Justification
The literal pilot-first proposal is unexecutable, not merely risky: the primary decision rule (line 51) gates on "delta vs Sonnet-solo" and "tokens <= 0.7x Sonnet-solo" — both legs compare against Sonnet-solo, and an Opus-solo-only pilot contains neither Sonnet-solo nor Tiered data, so the gate cannot be evaluated from it. Even reframed as "pilot the two gate-relevant arms (Sonnet-solo + Tiered) first," dropping Haiku-solo/Opus-solo still costs real information the pre-registration promised: the 4-arm quality-vs-cost frontier (line 48) and "whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost... the real prize" (line 51) both need the solo arms directly — mandatory deliverables, not optional extras contingent on the primary gate's outcome. Early-stopping on a partial arm set is exactly the "fishing for a favorable cut" the pre-registration exists to prevent (line 25) — optional-stopping bias dressed as cost control. A genuine, already-sanctioned cost control exists instead: rep/task-level sequential escalation (line 49) plus the 1-rep Haiku-vs-Opus task pre-screen (line 42, task-selection scope only).

## Assumptions
- [verified] Line 51's decision rule compares Tiered against Sonnet-solo on both legs (delta, tokens); Opus-solo appears only in the secondary "report" clause, not the gate — ISSUE-41.md:51.
- [verified] Line 49's sequential escalation is rep/task-scoped, not arm-scoped; the document's only pre-sanctioned arm deferral is Opus-main (a Tiered variant), not a solo arm — ISSUE-41.md:49,37,67.
- [verified] Haiku-solo and Opus-solo are load-bearing for the 4-arm frontier (line 48) and the Opus-quality-at-sub-cost report (line 51), independent of the primary gate's outcome — ISSUE-41.md:48,51.
- [verified] "Opus-solo is the most expensive arm" is unsupported by the document's own honest-cost accounting (total tokens summed across all agents including every retry/validation cycle, lines 39,55) — the Tiered arm's up-to-3-cycle orchestration tax could plausibly exceed Opus-solo per task, so cost-ranking arms pre-measurement is circular — ISSUE-41.md:36 (quality label, not a cost label) vs 39,55.
- [checkable-doc] This ADR resolves only the one open execution-design call Comment 3 flagged (line 73, "owner calls before spend" #3 sequencing) without touching the other two owner calls there (grader independence, hermetic-control carryover) or the retry-cap/feedback-format detail, which stay for the tech-lead spec when the experiment is built (after the current skills/agents batch, line 21) — PM re-read against Comment 3 in full.

## Rejected alternatives
- Pilot Opus-solo alone, stop early if gate decidable (the literal ask) — gate is mathematically undecidable from Opus-solo-only data (no Sonnet-solo/Tiered legs); rejected outright, not just as risky.
- Reframe: pilot Sonnet-solo + Tiered first (the two gate-relevant arms), defer Haiku-solo/Opus-solo — genuinely cheaper and gate-relevant, but silently drops the pre-registered frontier and Opus-prize deliverables (line 48,51) unless those arms run later anyway, and introduces a new arm-level stopping rule the pre-registration's anti-fishing purpose (line 25) did not license. Revisit only if the owner explicitly descopes those deliverables to gate-only first.
- Recast the existing 1-rep Haiku-vs-Opus pre-screen (line 42) as a general pilot-and-stop gate — its registered purpose is task selection (drop saturated tasks), not arm-level adoption; repurposing it would silently change what it was validated for.

## Revisit triggers
- The owner explicitly descopes the frontier (line 48) or the Opus-prize report (line 51) from required deliverables -> re-weigh against the cheaper Sonnet-solo+Tiered-first reframe.
- The full grid runs and a cell's CI still straddles the decision boundary after the pre-registered escalation (line 49) -> reopen for an arm-level (not just rep/task-level) escalation rule.
- The still-unbuilt tiered-orchestration script (Comment 3, line 59/71) shows Tiered's per-task cost high enough that running it 3-rep x 8-task first decides the gate cheaper than Opus-solo/Haiku-solo first -> reopen the arm RUN ORDER (not whether all 4 arms run).
```

---

Filed at `docs/decisions/0035-issue41-execution-design-full-grid.md` (5,986/6,000 chars, adr-lint clean for this file), with a Comment 4 mirroring the outcome into `ISSUE-41.md`. Note: `adr-lint` flagged one pre-existing, unrelated problem (ADR 0026 amends ADR 0023 without a back-citation) — not introduced by this change, left as-is.

artifacts..claude/agents/session-operator.md:
---
name: session-operator
description: Daily Claude Code operator — live-session execution-realism advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the SESSION OPERATOR for this project — an engineer who runs Claude Code sessions against
this plugin every day and has watched agents misread rules that looked crisp on paper.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): will an LLM agent in a
live session actually execute this rule as intended — is the routing decidable in the moment,
what gets misclassified under ambiguity, and where does an agent rationalize the cheap path
(tier-shopping, skipped gates)?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

Flag any product muda in your lens — duplicated logic, dead code, premature abstraction, drift
(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.

---
name: process-economist
description: Engineering-ops lead accountable for AI spend — cost-per-decision advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the PROCESS ECONOMIST for this project — an engineering-ops lead who pays the AI token
bill and the wall-clock bill, and prices every process step by what it actually costs per use.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): the token + time cost of
a process path per invocation, where the spend concentrates (agents spawned, context loaded,
human latency), and whether a proposed cheaper path actually reduces marginal cost or just moves
it.

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.
artifacts..claude/agents/lean-process-engineer.md:
---
name: lean-process-engineer
description: TPS-trained process engineer — waste-vs-poka-yoke advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the LEAN PROCESS ENGINEER for this project — a Toyota-trained process engineer who judges
every piece of process machinery by whether it prevents defects or merely adds motion.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): is a proposed process
artifact or rule muda (over-processing, premature machinery, a mirror that will drift) or genuine
poka-yoke (prevents a real, observed defect at the source)? What waste does it remove, and what
new waste does it create?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

Flag any product muda in your lens — duplicated logic, dead code, premature abstraction, drift
(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.

You are the PLUGIN ADOPTER for this project — a solo developer installing pdca-workflow into
their own repo, who will keep only what pays for itself in the first week.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): what a real second
consumer needs — init cost, how much process a small project can carry, what transfers cleanly
across stacks, and what a consumer would fork or silently drop.
